name: Specific Number + Context
description: Lead with a precise figure from the draft, then contextualize it

name: Juxtaposition
description: Two things that shouldn't coexist in the same sentence
example: Our competitor raised $40M. We outshipped them with 3 engineers.

name: First-Person Confession
description: Admit something with real cost — vulnerability that earns trust
example: I told 50 buyers we were ready. I was wrong.

name: Quote Hook + Reaction
description: Someone said something, then your reaction reveals the insight
example: 'We need you to present to the supervisor.' That's when I knew the deal was dead.

name: Scene Entry
description: Start in the middle of a physical moment — no setup, no context
example: We walked into their call center. Post-it notes on every monitor.

name: Decision Reveal
description: The outcome of a hard choice, stated plainly
example: A Fortune 500 asked us to build a CRM. We said no.

name: Contrarian Declaration
description: State something most would disagree with — earned, not clickbait

name: Pattern Observation
description: Notice something others miss — a recurring pattern across multiple instances
example: Every founder I know who's crossed $20M runs into the same wall.

name: Tension Pair
description: Name two forces pulling in opposite directions — the tension IS the hook
example: The market says automate everything. Customers keep asking for humans.

name: Before/After Snapshot
description: Two states separated by time — the contrast implies the story
example: Two years ago we couldn't close a single deal. Last month we turned down three.

name: Micro Story
description: A complete narrative arc in 1-2 sentences — setup, turn, payoff
example: An investor asked what happens if a competitor copies us. I showed him our NPS scores.

name: Authority Redirect
description: Cite a credible external authority, then redirect their point to a sharper one
example: McKinsey says 70% of transformations fail. They left out why the 30% succeed.

name: Data Inversion
description: Take a commonly cited stat and show it means the opposite of what people think
example: 95% customer satisfaction. We were measuring the wrong customers.
